title: Estate agent — viewing request
description: Arrange a viewing with proceedability signals in one short email.
bodyTemplate:
Hi {{contact.agentName}},

I'd like to arrange a viewing of {{listing.address}} (listed at £{{listing.askingPrice}}, {{listing.portal}} ref {{listing.ref}}).

Position: first-time buyer, chain-free, AIP in place for £{{finances.aipAmount}}.

Could either {{viewingDateOption1}} or {{viewingDateOption2}} work? I can also do most evenings after 5pm.

Many thanks,
{{profile.firstName}} {{profile.lastName}}
{{profile.mobile}}
bestPracticeNotes: Mentions proceedability in one line — earns priority in the agent's pile
Offers two specific time slots — research shows this triples the reply rate vs. 'when suits'
Includes the listing reference so the agent can find the property instantly

sources.title: Avocado Property — Setting up a viewing

title: Estate agent — saved-search registration
description: Introduce yourself so the agent calls you first when a matching property comes to market.
bestPracticeNotes: Agents prioritise registered buyers with AIP — you move to the top of their call list
Listing specific criteria saves agents filtering unsuitable properties at you
Saying 'I reply promptly' signals you are a low-friction buyer — agents prefer easy completions

title: Estate agent — pre-viewing question batch
description: Get answers to the 10 key questions before spending time on a viewing.
bodyTemplate:
Hi {{contact.agentName}},

Before I book a viewing of {{listing.address}}, could you confirm a few things?

1. Chain: is the vendor already in a chain, or are they chain-free?
2. Time on market: how long has the property been listed?
3. Offers: have any offers already been made or declined?
4. EPC: what is the current energy rating?
5. Council tax: which band?
6. Any structural disclosures — damp, subsidence, planning refusals, boundary disputes?
7. Parking: is there off-street parking included?
8. Broadband: what is the current speed/type available?
9. Last sold: when and for how much?
10. Reason for sale?

Many thanks — happy to book a viewing once I have these.

{{profile.firstName}} {{profile.lastName}}
{{profile.mobile}}
bestPracticeNotes: Gathering this before viewing saves a wasted trip and signals you're a serious, prepared buyer
Structural disclosures — asked in writing — are harder for agents to dismiss later
Reason for sale and time on market inform your offer strategy before you set foot inside

sources.title: HomeOwners Alliance — 25 questions to ask the estate agent

title: Vendor direct — off-market enquiry
description: A polite, human letter to a homeowner you'd love to buy from.
bodyTemplate:
Hello,

I hope you don't mind a short letter from a stranger. My name is {{profile.firstName}} — I've been looking for a home in {{vendor.areaName}} for several months and I love your street.

I'm a first-time buyer, chain-free, with a mortgage agreement already in place. I'm not from an agency and there's no fee involved — I'd simply love to know if you've ever thought about selling, now or in the next year. If the timing is wrong, I completely understand — please feel free to disregard this.

If it's a 'maybe one day', I'd really value a brief chat. You can reach me on {{profile.mobile}} or {{profile.email}} — no obligation, no follow-up unless you'd like one.

With thanks,
{{profile.firstName}}
bestPracticeNotes: First-name only — you're writing to someone's home, not a transaction
Opt-out language up front earns the right to be read
No price, no specifics — that conversation comes if they reply
Avoids spam-trigger phrases ('cash buyer', 'will pay any price') that read as wholesaler outreach

sources.title: Agent Fire — Cold email guide for real estate leads

title: Mortgage broker — initial enquiry
description: Full intro to a broker with all the numbers they need to advise you.
bodyTemplate:
Hi {{contact.brokerName}},

I'm looking for first-time buyer mortgage advice and would like to set up an initial call.

Quick context so you can come prepared:
- Solo applicant, no dependents, excellent credit, no adverse history.
- Permanent employment: £{{finances.income.annualBaseSalary}} base + ~£{{finances.income.annualBonus}} average bonus.
- Target property price: £{{finances.goal.targetPropertyPrice}} (offer target £{{finances.goal.offerTarget}}).
- Deposit: £{{finances.goal.targetDeposit}} ({{finances.goal.depositPct}}% LTV target).
- Saving £{{finances.savings.monthlyContribution}}/month; currently £{{finances.savings.current}} saved.
- Moving window: {{finances.goal.movingWindow}}.
- Preference: {{finances.mortgage.fixedRatePref}} fixed, willing to pay £{{finances.mortgage.feesWillingness.max}} max in arrangement fees.
- Search area: Hampshire (near Winchester/Salisbury), countryside villages.

Could we book a 30-minute call this week or next? Tuesday/Thursday afternoons work well for me, but I'm flexible.

Thanks,
{{profile.firstName}} {{profile.lastName}}
{{profile.mobile}} · {{profile.email}}
bestPracticeNotes: Brokers triage by completeness — listing all the numbers up front saves a back-and-forth
Specifying credit profile and FTB status sets expectations for product range
Suggested call slots beat 'whenever suits'

title: Mortgage broker — AIP renewal
description: Request a refreshed Agreement in Principle before yours expires.
bodyTemplate:
Hi {{contact.brokerName}},

My current Agreement in Principle from {{finances.aipLender}} is due to expire on {{finances.aipExpiryDate}}. Could you please initiate a renewal?

My circumstances are unchanged:
- Permanent employment, same employer.
- Income: £{{finances.income.annualBaseSalary}} base, unchanged.
- Deposit now at £{{finances.savings.current}} saved.
- Still targeting £{{finances.goal.targetPropertyPrice}} in Hampshire/Wiltshire, moving window {{finances.goal.movingWindow}}.

I'm actively viewing properties and would like a refreshed AIP before I make an offer.

Thanks,
{{profile.firstName}} {{profile.lastName}}
{{profile.mobile}}
bestPracticeNotes: AIPs expire at 60–90 days — renewing proactively avoids a gap when you need to move fast on an offer
Confirming circumstances unchanged saves a full re-interview
Brokers can often renew on a soft search if nothing has changed

title: Estate agent — formal offer (with mortgage)
description: The Charlie Lamdin 'First and Final' offer structure, adapted for FTB.
bodyTemplate:
Dear {{contact.agentName}},

Subject to survey and contract.

I'd like to make a formal offer to purchase {{listing.address}}. Please acknowledge receipt of this offer by return, and confirm once it has been passed to the seller.

Offer amount: £{{listing.offerAmount}}{{#if listing.offerAsPctOfAsking}} ({{listing.offerAsPctOfAsking}}% of the £{{listing.askingPrice}} asking price){{/if}}.

My position:
- First-time buyer, chain-free, no property to sell.
- Agreement in Principle from {{finances.aipLender}} for up to £{{finances.aipAmount}} — attached.
- Deposit of £{{finances.depositAmount}} held in {{finances.depositSource}} — screenshot attached.
- Conveyancer instructed: {{contact.solicitorFirm}}, {{contact.solicitorEmail}}.
- Target exchange within 8–12 weeks of acceptance; happy to discuss a specific completion date.

This is not an opening bid. It reflects the maximum I am comfortable paying for this property at current market conditions. If it is not acceptable, I will respectfully withdraw and continue my search.

I'd appreciate a response by {{offerDeadline}}. If I don't hear back by then I'll assume the offer is declined and reallocate my attention.

Best regards,
{{profile.firstName}} {{profile.lastName}}
{{profile.mobile}} · {{profile.email}}
{{profile.postcode}}
attachmentsHint: Solicitor's letter of engagement (optional but powerful)
bestPracticeNotes: States proceedability up front — the only thing the agent forwards to the seller
Anchors the offer as a final, not opening bid — reduces back-and-forth
Time-boxed deadline forces agent action; protects you from being held while other offers are sought
Subject-to-survey-and-contract is non-negotiable boilerplate

sources.title: Moving Home With Charlie — FTB Offer Template

title: Estate agent — counter-offer response
description: Respond to a counter-offer: accept, revise or decline clearly.
bestPracticeNotes: Reaffirming your position in every counter-offer email reminds the seller you're a proceedable buyer
A written response creates a paper trail — useful if the agent misrepresents the exchange
Requesting confirmation of receipt triggers Property Ombudsman obligations on the agent

title: Estate agent — offer withdrawal
description: Withdraw an offer cleanly if research surfaces a deal-breaker post-viewing.
bodyTemplate:
Dear {{contact.agentName}},

I am writing to formally withdraw my offer of £{{listing.offerAmount}} on {{listing.address}}.

Reason: {{withdrawalReason}}.

Please confirm receipt and pass this withdrawal to the seller. I wish them well in finding a buyer.

Kind regards,
{{profile.firstName}} {{profile.lastName}}
{{profile.mobile}}
bestPracticeNotes: Written withdrawal creates a clear end-point — protects you if the agent later claims the offer stood
Providing a reason (briefly) is professional and helps maintain goodwill with the agent
Always withdraw in writing, not just verbally — verbal withdrawals are frequently disputed

sources.title: Compare My Move — Offer accepted on a house

title: Estate agent — confirm offer was conveyed
description: Written nudge to confirm the agent passed your offer to the seller (Property Ombudsman obligation).
bodyTemplate:
Dear {{contact.agentName}},

I made a formal offer of £{{listing.offerAmount}} on {{listing.address}} on {{offerDate}}. Could you please confirm:

1. The offer has been passed to the seller.
2. The seller has been informed of all the details including my buyer position.
3. The current status of the seller's decision.

As you'll be aware, estate agents are required under the Property Ombudsman Code of Practice to pass all offers to the seller promptly and in writing. I would appreciate your written confirmation.

Kind regards,
{{profile.firstName}} {{profile.lastName}}
{{profile.mobile}} · {{profile.email}}
bestPracticeNotes: Property Ombudsman Code obliges agents to pass all offers — this email invokes that obligation in writing
Creates a paper trail if you later need to complain about an offer not being presented
Firm but polite — agents who know you understand the rules tend to act faster

sources.title: Property Buyers Today — Proof of funds
First Keys Toolkit — After offer accepted

title: Estate agent — request property removed from market
description: Anti-gazumping: formally request the property is marked Sold Subject to Contract.
bodyTemplate:
Dear {{contact.agentName}},

Following the acceptance of my offer of £{{listing.agreedPrice}} on {{listing.address}}, I'd like to formally request that the property is now marked as Sold Subject to Contract and removed from active marketing on all portals.

Gazumping is a known risk at this stage. Continuing to market the property exposes both parties to delays and failed sales — not in anyone's interest.

Could you confirm once this has been done?

Kind regards,
{{profile.firstName}} {{profile.lastName}}
{{profile.mobile}} · {{profile.email}}
bestPracticeNotes: Agents are not legally obliged to remove a property from market — but this written request creates accountability
Referencing both parties' interests frames this as a collaborative ask, not a demand
If the agent refuses, you have a documented signal that may inform your risk appetite

sources.title: Boon Brokers — Offer accepted next steps

title: Mortgage broker — proceed with full application
description: Instruct the broker to progress to a full mortgage application now an offer is accepted.
attachmentsHint: Proof of ID and address (passport + utility bill)
bestPracticeNotes: Sharing your solicitor's details up front saves days of back-and-forth between broker and conveyancer
Including the target exchange date creates a shared timeline to work towards
Asking 'what do you need from me' shifts responsibility for the next step to the broker

sources.title: Compare My Move — Offer accepted on a house

title: Solicitor — conveyancing quote request
description: Request a like-for-like quote from a conveyancer with six specific questions.
bodyTemplate:
Dear Sirs,

I'd like to request a conveyancing quote for the purchase of {{listing.address}}, agreed at £{{listing.agreedPrice}}.

Key details:
- Tenure: {{listing.tenure}}.
- Property type: {{listing.propertyType}}, {{listing.beds}}-bed.
- New build: {{listing.isNewBuild}}.
- Mortgage lender: {{finances.aipLender}} (subject to full application).
- I am a first-time buyer, chain-free.
- Target exchange: 8–12 weeks from offer acceptance ({{offerAcceptedDate}}).

Please could your quote include:
1. Total fees including VAT, broken down by stage.
2. All disbursements (Land Registry, searches, SDLT submission, AML).
3. Whether you offer a no-sale-no-fee arrangement, and what disbursements remain payable if so.
4. The name of the solicitor/conveyancer who will handle my file day-to-day.
5. Whether you are on {{finances.aipLender}}'s panel.
6. Your CLC / SRA regulation number and complaints procedure.

Many thanks,
{{profile.firstName}} {{profile.lastName}}
{{profile.mobile}} · {{profile.email}}
bestPracticeNotes: The six numbered questions force apples-to-apples quotes between firms
Confirming lender panel up front prevents 'panel fee' surprises after instruction
Naming your day-to-day contact prevents being handed to a junior post-instruction

sources.title: Barratt London — Questions to ask your solicitor

title: Solicitor — formal instruction
description: Formally instruct your chosen conveyancer with all the details they need to open your file.
attachmentsHint: Signed client care letter / letter of engagement
Proof of address (utility bill or bank statement, dated within 3 months)
Proof of deposit source (bank statements or gift letter)
bestPracticeNotes: Sharing seller's solicitor details at instruction speeds up the memorandum of sale
Introducing broker and agent in the same email reduces solicitor admin time
Stating the target exchange date from day one creates a shared goal

sources.title: HomeOwners Alliance — Offer accepted next steps
First Keys Toolkit — After offer accepted

title: Surveyor — RICS Level 2 quote request
description: Request a HomeBuyer Report quote with access details and specific concerns.
bodyTemplate:
Hi,

I'd like a quote for a RICS Level 2 HomeBuyer Report on {{listing.address}}, including a market valuation if priced as a small add-on.

Property details:
- Type: {{listing.propertyType}}, {{listing.beds}}-bed.
- Approx age / construction: {{listing.age}} / {{listing.construction}}.
- Agreed purchase price: £{{listing.agreedPrice}}.
- Listing: {{listing.url}}.
- Specific concerns I'd like the report to flag: {{surveyConcerns}}.

For booking, the access contact is:
- {{contact.agentName}}, {{contact.agentEmail}}, {{contact.agentPhone}}.

I'd like the survey carried out within 2 weeks if possible. Could you confirm:
1. Your fee inc. VAT.
2. Earliest available date.
3. Turnaround for the report after inspection.
4. Whether a 15-minute follow-up call with the surveyor is included.

Thanks,
{{profile.firstName}} {{profile.lastName}}
{{profile.mobile}}
bestPracticeNotes: Naming the access contact saves a day of back-and-forth
Asking for the follow-up call up front avoids being charged extra later
Specifying concerns sharpens the report — surveyors know where to look

title: Surveyor — booking confirmation
description: Confirm the chosen surveyor and access arrangement for the inspection.
bestPracticeNotes: Giving two date options avoids a scheduling back-and-forth
Letting the agent know to expect the surveyor's call prevents access delays
Confirming the turnaround period in writing holds the surveyor to the quoted timeframe

title: Estate agent — share solicitor and broker contacts
description: Send your professional contacts to the agent for the memorandum of sale.
bestPracticeNotes: The memorandum of sale can't be issued until the agent has both sets of details — sending proactively saves days
A single email with all contacts prevents the agent chasing each party separately
Getting this done quickly reduces the window for gazumping

sources.title: Boon Brokers — Offer accepted next steps
Compare My Move — Offer accepted on a house

title: Solicitor — progress chase
description: A respectful weekly nudge to keep your file moving forward.
bodyTemplate:
Dear {{contact.solicitorName}},

I hope you're well. I'm writing for a brief progress update on the purchase of {{listing.address}}.

Could you confirm:
1. Where we are in the process (searches, enquiries, mortgage offer).
2. Any outstanding items you're waiting on — and from whom.
3. Your current estimate for exchange and completion.

I'm keen to keep things moving and happy to chase any third parties if it helps. Please let me know if there's anything you need from me.

Many thanks,
{{profile.firstName}} {{profile.lastName}}
{{profile.mobile}}
bestPracticeNotes: A weekly written chase (not phone call) creates a timestamped record of your engagement
Offering to chase third parties shifts perceived burden — solicitors often welcome it
Asking for an exchange estimate surfaces any hidden delays early

sources.title: HomeOwners Alliance — Offer accepted next steps

title: Surveyor — request post-report call
description: Request a 15-minute walk-through of your RICS Level 2 report findings.
bestPracticeNotes: RICS Level 2 reports use standard traffic-light ratings — a 15-min call clarifies whether 'amber' means 'monitor' or 'renegotiate'
Listing your questions up front lets the surveyor prepare and makes the call efficient
This call is often free if agreed in the booking; if not, negotiate a small fixed fee

title: Estate agent — renegotiate after survey
description: Request a price reduction based on specific surveyor-quoted repair costs.
attachmentsHint: RICS Level 2 report (relevant sections or full report)
Surveyor's cost estimate if provided separately
bestPracticeNotes: Citing specific surveyor-quoted figures (not estimates you've made) gives the renegotiation credibility
Reaffirming commitment to the purchase reduces seller anxiety — you want a reduction, not an exit
Attaching the relevant report sections prevents the seller dismissing findings as buyer invention

sources.title: HomeOwners Alliance — Offer accepted next steps
Moving Home With Charlie — FTB Offer Template

title: Solicitor — request exchange target / expedite
description: Set a concrete exchange target date and ask what's blocking it.
bodyTemplate:
Dear {{contact.solicitorName}},

I'd like to propose {{targetExchangeDate}} as our target exchange date for {{listing.address}}, with a completion on {{targetCompletionDate}}.

Could you confirm:
1. Whether this timeline is achievable from your side.
2. Any outstanding enquiries or searches that are blocking exchange.
3. Whether the seller's solicitor has confirmed their readiness.

If there are specific items holding things up, please let me know and I'll do whatever I can to help move things forward.

Kind regards,
{{profile.firstName}} {{profile.lastName}}
{{profile.mobile}}
bestPracticeNotes: Proposing a specific exchange date shifts from open-ended to time-bounded — solicitors respond better to deadlines
Asking about seller's solicitor readiness surfaces the most common source of delay
Written exchange target creates mutual accountability between both sets of solicitors

sources.title: Compare My Move — Offer accepted on a house

title: Removals — quote request
description: Request a like-for-like removals quote from three firms.
bodyTemplate:
Hi,

I'm looking for a removals quote for a house move.

Move details:
- From: {{profile.address}} ({{profile.postcode}})
- To: {{listing.address}}
- Target move date: around {{targetCompletionDate}} (subject to exchange — I'll confirm once a date is set).
- Volume: {{removalsVolume}} (approx {{removalsRooms}} bedrooms).
- Large / specialist items: {{removalsSpecialItems}}.
- Packing: {{removalsPackingReq}}.

Could you provide a fixed-price quote including VAT, and confirm whether you offer a date-change policy if completion moves?

Thanks,
{{profile.firstName}} {{profile.lastName}}
{{profile.mobile}} · {{profile.email}}
bestPracticeNotes: Send to three firms simultaneously — prices can vary by 40% for identical moves
Asking about a date-change policy is critical since completion dates often shift at the last minute
Specifying large/specialist items up front prevents surprise surcharges on the day

sources.title: Compare My Move — Offer accepted on a house

title: Buildings insurance — quote request
description: Request buildings insurance from exchange day — required by your mortgage lender.
bestPracticeNotes: Cover must begin at exchange, not completion — lenders require this and many buyers miss the gap
Using the RICS rebuild cost (not market value) ensures correct cover and avoids over-insurance
Confirming lender acceptability up front avoids having to switch policy under pressure

sources.title: Boon Brokers — Offer accepted next steps
Compare My Move — Offer accepted on a house

title: Utilities — change of occupier notification
description: Notify energy, water, council tax and broadband suppliers on completion day.
attachmentsHint: Photos of meter readings taken on completion day
bestPracticeNotes: Send to: energy supplier, water company, council tax office, broadband provider, TV Licence
Taking meter readings on completion day prevents being billed for the seller's usage
Use this same template for each supplier — only the opening paragraph needs to change

sources.title: Compare My Move — Offer accepted on a house
